content.text: Long documents, FAQs reused over hours

Cache created at 10:00:00
→ Accessed at 10:02:00 → TTL refreshed to 10:07:00
→ Accessed at 10:05:00 → TTL refreshed to 10:10:00
→ Accessed at 10:09:00 → TTL refreshed to 10:14:00
The cache lifetime is refreshed each time the cached content is accessed

Cache created at 10:00:00
→ No access until 10:06:00 → Cache expires (6 min > 5 min TTL)
→ Next request at 10:07:00 → Must re-write entire prefix (no cache hit)
Pay 125% write cost again instead of 10% read cost.

5-minute write:  1M × $3.75 = $3.75
1-hour write:    1M × $6.00 = $6.00

For documents reused over hours, 1-hour cache is worth the extra cost.
Unique to your Google Cloud project; cannot be used by other projects
Occurs when requests contain identical text, images, and
Each time cached content is accessed
Case Study: Cache TTL Regression (1 Hour → 5 Minutes)
Claude's prompt caching TTL silently dropped from

Original (1-hour TTL):
- Cache expires after hours of inactivity
- Suitable for most API workflows [web:43]

Regression (5-minute TTL):
- Cache expires after one short distraction
- Near-constant re-write expense [web:43]
- Developers paying 25% surcharge repeatedly instead of 90% discount [web:43]
"A 5-minute TTL means the cache expires after one short distraction, turning what should be a cost-saving feature into a near-constant re-write expense".
Monitoring Cache TTL in Claude Code

# Green: cache warm (< 3 min since last API call)
# Yellow: getting stale (3-4 min)
# Red: about to expire (4-5 min)
# Red + "expired": cache gone, consider starting fresh
Timer tracks last API call as a proxy, not exact server-side cache status.
For documents reused over hours, 1-hour cache is worth the extra 100% surcharge.
— 3.7 Sonnet, 3.5 Sonnet, 3 Opus don't support it
— prevents expiration if reused within TTL window
— silently dropped from 1 hour in April 2026, causing constant re-write costs
— color-coded status line shows cache freshness in Claude Code
Production experience with cache lifetime & ttl has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Making informed architectural decisions requires concrete numbers. These benchmarks are representative of typical production workloads.
Claude Haiku 3.5: ~$0.80/MTok input, ~$4/MTok output. Best for high-volume, simple tasks like classification, extraction, and summarization.
Claude Sonnet 4: ~$3/MTok input, ~$15/MTok output. The best quality-to-cost ratio for most production use cases requiring reasoning.
Prompt cache hit rate: After the first call, 85-95% of requests hit the cache for stable prompts. Cache tokens cost 10% of normal input token price.
Message Batches API: 50% cost reduction for async workloads processed within 24 hours. Ideal for nightly data processing, evaluation runs, and bulk content generation.
Typical latency: 1-3s for Haiku, 3-10s for Sonnet, 10-30s for Opus. Streaming makes all models feel 2-5x faster from the user's perspective.
Deploying cache lifetime & ttl in production requires careful attention to security boundaries and trust models.
API key management — store keys in environment variables or secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault). Never commit to version control.
Prompt injection prevention — when embedding user input in prompts, use XML tags to clearly delimit user content from instructions, reducing injection attack surface.
Output sanitization — before rendering AI-generated content in web UIs, escape HTML to prevent XSS attacks from model-produced content.
Rate limiting user requests — implement per-user rate limits in addition to API limits to prevent cost abuse and ensure fair service distribution.

Cache Lifetime & TTL is a foundational capability for building production-ready Claude integrations. By following the patterns in this guide — proper error handling, async clients, prompt caching, and structured monitoring — you can build reliable, cost-efficient applications that scale gracefully from prototype to production.
Production experience with cache lifetime & ttl has surfaced these practices that consistently improve reliability and performance.
Use async clients for concurrent workloads — Python's AsyncAnthropic and TypeScript's async/await patterns avoid blocking the event loop during API calls.
Cache system prompts with cache_control — stable system prompts hit the cache ~90% of the time after the first call, reducing both latency and cost significantly.
Stream responses for interactive use — streaming reduces time-to-first-token from seconds to milliseconds, dramatically improving perceived responsiveness.
Implement structured retry logic — use exponential backoff starting at 1 second, with jitter to prevent thundering herd problems under rate limiting.
Log structured metadata — capture model, input_tokens, output_tokens, latency, and request_id in every log entry for effective debugging and cost tracking.
Set explicit max_tokens — always specify max_tokens to bound cost and prevent unexpectedly long responses that consume your token budget.
Awareness of these common failure modes prevents hours of debugging in production.
Not validating response structure — even when you request JSON, parse it with a try/catch. Models occasionally produce malformed output under high load or edge-case inputs.
Accumulating conversation history without truncation — after 10-20 turns, conversations can exceed context windows. Implement a sliding window or summarization strategy.
Mixing prompt concerns — put behavior instructions in system prompts, task-specific content in user turns. Mixing them reduces clarity and makes iteration harder.
Ignoring stop_reason — check if stop_reason is "max_tokens" rather than "end_turn". Truncated responses need handling differently than complete ones.
Creating new client instances per request — instantiate the Anthropic client once at module load time to benefit from connection pooling across requests.
Reading Cache Efficiency from the API Response

Every Claude API response includes a usage object that tells you whether your cache worked. Understanding these fields is essential for diagnosing cache misses and confirming that your TTL configuration is behaving as expected.

The key distinction is between cache_creation_input_tokens and cache_read_input_tokens. A write means the request created a new cache entry and incurred the write surcharge. A read means a prior cache entry was reused and you paid only the heavily discounted read rate. If both are zero, the request was not eligible for caching at all — which can happen when the cached content falls below the minimum threshold or when the prompt structure is not cacheable.
Monitoring Cache Hit Rate in Production

Tracking cache hit rate over time lets you know whether your TTL strategy is working. A high cache write rate with few reads suggests your TTL is too short, your call frequency is too low, or your prompt content is changing too often between calls.

import anthropic
from collections import defaultdict

class CacheMonitor:
    def __init__(self):
        self.stats = defaultdict(int)

    def record(self, usage):
        if usage.cache_read_input_tokens > 0:
            self.stats['hits'] += 1
            self.stats['read_tokens'] += usage.cache_read_input_tokens
        elif usage.cache_creation_input_tokens > 0:
            self.stats['writes'] += 1
            self.stats['write_tokens'] += usage.cache_creation_input_tokens
        else:
            self.stats['uncached'] += 1
        self.stats['total_requests'] += 1

    def report(self):
        total = self.stats['total_requests']
        hits = self.stats['hits']
        writes = self.stats['writes']
        hit_rate = hits / total if total > 0 else 0
        print(f"Total requests:  {total}")
        print(f"Cache hits:      {hits} ({hit_rate:.1%})")
        print(f"Cache writes:    {writes}")
        print(f"Uncached:        {self.stats['uncached']}")
        print(f"Read tokens:     {self.stats['read_tokens']:,}")
        print(f"Write tokens:    {self.stats['write_tokens']:,}")

monitor = CacheMonitor()
# Pass usage from each response into monitor.record(response.usage)
Cost Modeling Across Different Usage Patterns
The right TTL choice depends heavily on your actual usage pattern. Understanding cost at different call frequencies helps you decide whether the 1-hour premium is worthwhile for your workflow.

System prompt: 50,000 tokens
Requests per hour: 120

5-minute TTL:
  Write cost: one write at start (cache refreshes on every access, never expires)
  Total write cost: 50,000 x $3.75/MTok = $0.1875
  Total read cost: 119 x 50,000 x $0.30/MTok = $1.785
  Total: $1.97

1-hour TTL:
  Write cost: 50,000 x $6.00/MTok = $0.30 (once per hour)
  Read cost: 119 x 50,000 x $0.30/MTok = $1.785
  Total: $2.085

Verdict: 5-minute TTL is cheaper for high-frequency use (cache never expires, lower write cost).
Scenario B: Slow document review (every 15 minutes)

System prompt: 200,000 tokens
Reviews per hour: 4 (every 15 min)

5-minute TTL:
  Each gap is 15 minutes => cache expires before reuse
  Write cost per request: 200,000 x $3.75/MTok = $0.75
  Total writes: 4 x $0.75 = $3.00 (no reads at all)

1-hour TTL:
  Write cost once: 200,000 x $6.00/MTok = $1.20
  Read cost: 3 x 200,000 x $0.30/MTok = $0.18
  Total: $1.38

Verdict: 1-hour TTL saves 54% when calls are spaced more than 5 minutes apart.
Scenario C: Batch overnight processing with two runs

System prompt: 500,000 tokens (large policy document)
Two batch runs, 6 hours apart

5-minute TTL:
  Each run writes fresh: 2 x 500,000 x $3.75/MTok = $3.75 total writes
  Within each run (100 requests in 20 min): 99 reads per batch
  Read savings per batch: 99 x 500,000 x ($3.00 - $0.30)/MTok = $133.65 savings

1-hour TTL:
  First run: 1 write ($3.00) + 99 reads
  Second run (6h later): 1 write ($3.00) + 99 reads (cache expired after 1h)
  Net cost same as 5-min TTL for 6-hour gap

Verdict: For batch runs more than 1h apart, neither TTL carries the cache across runs.
Design for within-batch caching rather than across-run caching.

The analysis shows that 1-hour TTL only pays off when your inter-request gap is regularly between 5 and 60 minutes. For gaps under 5 minutes, the 5-minute TTL is sufficient and cheaper. For gaps over 60 minutes, neither TTL avoids a re-write.
Designing Systems That Maximize Cache Reuse

Effective prompt caching is not just about which TTL you pick — it is about structuring your prompts so the stable, cacheable content is as large as possible and the dynamic, per-request content is as small as possible.

Claude caches from the beginning of the prompt. The golden rule is: put everything that does not change first, and everything that changes per request at the end. This maximizes the number of tokens that can be reused across calls.

Claude's API supports up to four cache control breakpoints per request, which allows you to cache different sections independently. This is valuable when your prompt has layers of stability — for example, a base system prompt that never changes, a tenant configuration that changes daily, and a retrieval context that changes per session.

Each breakpoint independently accumulates its own cache entry. If global instructions have not changed, that prefix is reused even if tenant policy or retrieved documents are new. This layered strategy can dramatically increase your effective cache hit rate.
Choosing the right cache TTL is a balance of write cost, expected hit rate, and workflow cadence. Use this framework to guide your decision.
Measure your actual inter-request gap — how much time typically passes between consecutive calls sharing the same large prefix?
If gap is under 5 minutes consistently: use 5-minute TTL (cheaper write, cache never expires in practice)
If gap is 5 to 60 minutes: use 1-hour TTL (extra write cost pays for itself after the first cache read)
If gap is over 60 minutes reliably: neither TTL helps across runs; cache only helps within a burst
For agentic workflows with unpredictable tool-call latency: default to 1-hour TTL
For batch workloads: use 1-hour TTL with identical cacheable blocks for best hit rates
Monitor cache_creation_input_tokens vs cache_read_input_tokens in production to validate your choice
Pre-warm caches before predictable traffic spikes using a minimal warm-up request
Use multiple cache breakpoints to cache layers with different lifetimes independently
